CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early — the LSTM-based meta-learner, joint initialization/update gate learning, and empirical competitiveness are made explicit. Notation appears consistent and the paper reports key experimental details (datasets, baselines, hyperparameters, confidence intervals) with effective figures/tables summarizing accuracy and test loss. Writing quality is good overall, though as a theory-minded reader I would have liked a clearer, more explicit explanation of the gradient-independence simplifying assumption and its implications; that caveat slightly reduces presentation clarity but does not obscure the main ideas. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a logical flow from problem setup to method and experiments, and its main contributions (LSTM-based meta-learner, learned initialization and update gates) are stated clearly early on. Notation appears consistent and definitions are provided, and figures/tables effectively support empirical claims on mini-ImageNet (including accuracy and test loss reporting). Writing quality is good overall, though a couple of experimental-detail sections (e.g., the gradient-independence assumption and exact episode sampling) could be described with slightly more clarity for full reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early — the LSTM-based meta-learner, joint initialization/update gate learning, and empirical competitiveness are made explicit. Notation appears consistent and the paper reports key experimental details (datasets, baselines, hyperparameters, confidence intervals) with effective figures/tables summarizing accuracy and test loss. Writing quality is good overall, though as a theory-minded reader I would have liked a clearer, more explicit explanation of the gradient-independence simplifying assumption and its implications; that caveat slightly reduces presentation clarity but does not obscure the main ideas.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a logical flow from problem setup to method and experiments, and its main contributions (LSTM-based meta-learner, learned initialization and update gates) are stated clearly early on. Notation appears consistent and definitions are provided, and figures/tables effectively support empirical claims on mini-ImageNet (including accuracy and test loss reporting). Writing quality is good overall, though a couple of experimental-detail sections (e.g., the gradient-independence assumption and exact episode sampling) could be described with slightly more clarity for full reproducibility.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a concrete new application of learned optimizers—a two-layer LSTM meta-learner that jointly learns a task-common initialization and a fixed sequence of update gates to rapidly train a CNN in few-shot episodes—which is a clear methodological novelty compared to standard metric-learning baselines. The technique meaningfully departs from existing matching- or metric-based few-shot approaches by learning an explicit optimization/update rule rather than embedding or similarity metrics, and the authors provide empirical comparisons to relevant baselines. That said, the idea is a natural extension of prior learned-optimizer/meta-learning work rather than a radical rethinking, and the lack of formal analysis of the learned update dynamics (noted in the summary) limits the degree of conceptual novelty. Overall the contribution is a notable and non-trivial advance but not a fundamentally new paradigm. | B(4): The paper introduces a notable and practically meaningful new idea—an LSTM-based meta-learner that outputs parameter updates and jointly learns a task-common initialization to enable rapid few-step adaptation—distinct from predominant metric-learning approaches like Matching Networks. Framed as a learned optimizer for few-shot classification, the technique is a non-trivial extension beyond existing baselines (it directly learns update gates and update sequences rather than fixed fine-tuning or nearest-neighbor rules). The contribution is clearly differentiated from prior work and would inform experts interested in learned update rules, though it stops short of a sweeping theoretical advance and is focused on a specific few-shot regime, so I view it as notable but not revolutionary.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a concrete new application of learned optimizers—a two-layer LSTM meta-learner that jointly learns a task-common initialization and a fixed sequence of update gates to rapidly train a CNN in few-shot episodes—which is a clear methodological novelty compared to standard metric-learning baselines. The technique meaningfully departs from existing matching- or metric-based few-shot approaches by learning an explicit optimization/update rule rather than embedding or similarity metrics, and the authors provide empirical comparisons to relevant baselines. That said, the idea is a natural extension of prior learned-optimizer/meta-learning work rather than a radical rethinking, and the lack of formal analysis of the learned update dynamics (noted in the summary) limits the degree of conceptual novelty. Overall the contribution is a notable and non-trivial advance but not a fundamentally new paradigm.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces a notable and practically meaningful new idea—an LSTM-based meta-learner that outputs parameter updates and jointly learns a task-common initialization to enable rapid few-step adaptation—distinct from predominant metric-learning approaches like Matching Networks. Framed as a learned optimizer for few-shot classification, the technique is a non-trivial extension beyond existing baselines (it directly learns update gates and update sequences rather than fixed fine-tuning or nearest-neighbor rules). The contribution is clearly differentiated from prior work and would inform experts interested in learned update rules, though it stops short of a sweeping theoretical advance and is focused on a specific few-shot regime, so I view it as notable but not revolutionary.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents clear empirical claims with code, hyperparameters, and reasonable baselines on mini-ImageNet, and that the LSTM meta-learner setup is practical. They also agree the gradient-independence approximation and the narrow evaluation (limited N and k regimes) are notable methodological limitations, and important baselines/ablations (e.g., MAML, learned-optimizer comparisons, stability across seeds) are missing. Because the empirical evidence is promising but these technical and comparative gaps limit the soundness of the claims, I rate it a 3.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents clear empirical claims and provides experimental evidence on mini-ImageNet with reported hyperparameters and released code, and compares to metric-learning baselines (Matching Networks) and simple finetune/NN baselines. However, there is no theoretical analysis or convergence guarantees for the learned LSTM update rule, and the authors rely on a gradient-independence simplification that ignores higher-order derivatives without sufficient justification. Important algorithmically similar baselines (e.g., MAML or prior learned-optimizer methods) and stronger ablations on stability across seeds and meta-update choices are missing, leaving some methodological gaps. Given these technical omissions, the results are promising but not fully substantiated, so I rate it a 3.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and backed by empirical evidence on mini-ImageNet with appropriate baselines (Matching Nets variants, nearest-neighbour, finetune), reporting accuracy and test-loss with confidence intervals and providing code and hyperparameters. The methodological setup (LSTM meta-learner producing parameter updates, episodic meta-training) is sound and practical, though the authors make a gradient-independence approximation (ignoring higher-order derivatives) that is a simplifying assumption and should be noted as a potential source of bias. The evaluation is somewhat narrow (only N=5, k={1,5}) so conclusions about broader regimes are limited, but these issues are minor and do not undermine the core experimental support for the claims.

main_claims: Proposes an LSTM-based meta-learner that learns an optimization/update rule to train a learner neural network for few-shot classification.
The meta-learner jointly learns a task-common initialization for the learner and a sequence of update gates (input/forget) enabling rapid convergence with a fixed small number of updates.
The proposed approach outperforms naive baselines and is competitive with state-of-the-art metric-learning approaches (Matching Networks) on few-shot benchmarks.
_summary.methodology: Train a two-layer LSTM meta-learner (second layer modified to output parameter updates) that, given preprocessed learner gradients and losses, produces parameter updates for a CNN classifier across a fixed small number of steps; the meta-learner is trained by optimizing the learner's test loss on episodes sampled from meta-training.
datasets: Mini-ImageNet (their constructed split of 100 ImageNet classes, 600 examples per class; 64/16/20 train/val/test)
limitations: Study focused on few-shot and few-classes settings (k=1 or 5, N=5); not evaluated across a full spectrum of training-example and class-count regimes.
Uses a gradient-independence simplifying assumption that ignores higher-order derivatives when computing meta-learner gradients.

reviewer_a.name: Theory-first Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Faculty in machine learning with a focus on optimization theory and formal guarantees for learning algorithms; has published theory papers on convergence and generalization of meta-learning methods.
traits: Penalises papers that propose new learned optimizers or update rules without formal analysis (proofs, convergence, or at least clear theoretical justification) — expects at least a sketch of why the learned rule converges or avoids pathological behavior.

Demands thorough ablations on key design choices that affect fundamental behavior (e.g., effect of number of meta-update steps, initialization sensitivity, and stability across seeds); will downgrade if these are missing or superficial.

Requires comparisons against algorithmically similar baselines (e.g., MAML, learned optimizers from prior literature) rather than only metric-learning baselines; lacking such comparisons reduces novelty/impact score.
Penalises insufficient reproducibility detail: missing hyperparameter lists, training schedules, random seeds, or code release plans lead to lower confidence and score.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Critical, focused on formal understanding and rigorous empirical controls; gives credit only when methods are accompanied by theoretical justification or very comprehensive ablations and reproducibility.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical Meta-learning Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher with extensive experience in few-shot and meta-learning for vision; publishes applied and empirical work that develops practical meta-learners and benchmarks them on standard tasks.
traits: Rewards novel, practically effective mechanisms for meta-learning (e.g., learned optimizers/LSTM meta-learners) that demonstrate improved few-shot performance even if formal analysis is limited.

Values strong empirical evidence on standard few-shot benchmarks (accuracy gains, learning speed, robustness across multiple held-out tasks) and will boost score if results are competitive with or improve over established baselines like Matching Networks and prototypical approaches.

Considers clarity and plausibility of experimental protocol and ablation sufficient for confidence (e.g., episode sampling, architecture details, held-out meta-test tasks); will penalise only if experiments are irreproducible or clearly flawed.

Favors methods that are practically simple and potentially generalize to varied architectures (e.g., LSTM-based update rules) and will reward demonstrations of rapid convergence with a fixed small number of updates.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and results-oriented: emphasizes solid empirical validation and practical contributions, and is willing to accept limited theory if experiments convincingly show usefulness.
